item.title: Code2LoRA: Hypernetwork-Generated Adapters for Code Language Models under Software Evolution, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using rag — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using rag — with real, executed output.

Code2LoRA: Hypernetwork-Generated Adapters for Code Language Models under Software Evolution keeps showing up in the feeds I track — and for good reason. The hard part is rarely the idea; it's the plumbing: wiring data, transforms, and models together without it turning into a pile of glue scripts. Recurring themes around this topic: rag, inference, fine-tuning, engineering, bot, search.

This is exactly where Pixeltable earns its keep. It gives you a declarative table where derived values are computed columns — define the transform once and it stays in sync as data arrives. No DAGs to wire, no batch jobs to babysit.

Semantic search over text with an embedding index

Retrieval is the backbone of RAG. Pixeltable lets you attach an embedding index to a column and run similarity search with a single call -- the embeddings are computed and indexed for you as data arrives.

import pixeltable as pxt

pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

t = pxt.create_table("demo.docs", {"id": pxt.Int, "text": pxt.String})
t.insert([
    {"id": 1, "text": "Pixeltable stores and transforms multimodal data."},
    {"id": 2, "text": "Cats are small domesticated carnivorous mammals."},
    {"id": 3, "text": "Vector search finds semantically similar documents."},
    {"id": 4, "text": "The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris, France."},
])

# A simple, deterministic, offline embedding so the demo needs no model files.
@pxt.udf
def toy_embed(s: str) -> pxt.Array[(16,), pxt.Float]:
    import numpy as np
    v = np.zeros(16, dtype=np.float32)
    for tok in s.lower().split():
        v[hash(tok) % 16] += 1.0
    n = np.linalg.norm(v)
    return v / n if n else v

t.add_embedding_index(column="text", string_embed=toy_embed)

sim = t.text.similarity("searching for similar text with vectors")
print(t.order_by(sim, asc=False).select(t.id, t.text, score=sim).limit(2).collect())

Run it and you get real output — here's the actual result captured from this exact snippet (ran in 6.45s):

id                                               text     score
0   4      The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris, France.  0.547645
1   1  Pixeltable stores and transforms multimodal data.  0.102019

That's not a mockup. The code above executed against Pixeltable and produced that table. Swap the toy pieces (a UDF, a local embedding) for production ones — a real model endpoint, a managed embedding — and the shape of your code doesn't change. That's the point.

Takeaways

  • Declarative beats imperative for data + AI: say what you want computed, not how to schedule it.
  • Computed columns stay in sync automatically as new rows land.
  • Multimodal is first-class — images, video, audio, and text live in the same table as your scalars.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Then drop the snippet above into a module and run it. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • What Building My Own AI Bot Taught Me About Generative AI
  • Gemini API File Search: Enhanced Multimodal Capabilities with Embedding 2, Including Open-Source LINE Bot Implementation
  • I made a personalized AI web app with RAG
  • An Oracle DBA builds AI: shipping Oracle 23ai RAG and an MCP server in a weekend
  • RAG Is Not Always the Answer Anymore: How AI Agents Search Code in 2026

item.title: Fine-tuning an LLM to write docs like it's 1995, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using llm — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using llm — with real, executed output.

Fine-tuning an LLM to write docs like it's 1995 keeps showing up in the feeds I track — and for good reason. The hard part is rarely the idea; it's the plumbing: wiring data, transforms, and models together without it turning into a pile of glue scripts. Recurring themes around this topic: llm, fine-tuning, agent, about, failing, because.

This is exactly where Pixeltable earns its keep. It gives you a declarative table where derived values are computed columns — define the transform once and it stays in sync as data arrives. No DAGs to wire, no batch jobs to babysit.

A declarative inference pipeline with a Python UDF

Wrap any function -- a heuristic, a local model, or an API call -- as a Pixeltable UDF and expose it as a computed column. The pipeline is declarative: define the transform once, and every current and future row flows through it automatically.

import pixeltable as pxt

pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

t = pxt.create_table("demo.prompts", {"id": pxt.Int, "prompt": pxt.String})
t.insert([
    {"id": 1, "prompt": "Summarize: Pixeltable is multimodal AI data infra."},
    {"id": 2, "prompt": "Translate to pirate: hello there"},
    {"id": 3, "prompt": "Classify sentiment: this library is fantastic"},
])

# Stand-in for a model call -- swap for an LLM endpoint in production.
@pxt.udf
def respond(prompt: str) -> str:
    kind = prompt.split(":")[0].strip().lower()
    return f"[{kind}] -> processed {len(prompt)} chars"

t.add_computed_column(response=respond(t.prompt))
print(t.select(t.id, t.response).collect())

Run it and you get real output — here's the actual result captured from this exact snippet (ran in 6.03s):

id                                     response
0   1            [summarize] -> processed 50 chars
1   2  [translate to pirate] -> processed 32 chars
2   3   [classify sentiment] -> processed 45 chars

That's not a mockup. The code above executed against Pixeltable and produced that table. Swap the toy pieces (a UDF, a local embedding) for production ones — a real model endpoint, a managed embedding — and the shape of your code doesn't change. That's the point.

Takeaways

  • Declarative beats imperative for data + AI: say what you want computed, not how to schedule it.
  • Computed columns stay in sync automatically as new rows land.
  • Multimodal is first-class — images, video, audio, and text live in the same table as your scalars.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Then drop the snippet above into a module and run it. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Engineering Agent Memory
  • An LLM API call, in 4 GIFs
  • I Added Three Rules to Gemma 4. The MoE Searched. The Dense Model Refused.
  • What Building My Own AI Bot Taught Me About Generative AI
  • Prompting Is Not Magic. It Is Control.

item.title: Gemma 4 12B: A unified, encoder-free multimodal model, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.

Gemma 4 12B: A unified, encoder-free multimodal model keeps showing up in the feeds I track — and for good reason. The hard part is rarely the idea; it's the plumbing: wiring data, transforms, and models together without it turning into a pile of glue scripts. Recurring themes around this topic: multimodal, rag, agent, qwen, 3.7, 2026.

This is exactly where Pixeltable earns its keep. It gives you a declarative table where derived values are computed columns — define the transform once and it stays in sync as data arrives. No DAGs to wire, no batch jobs to babysit.

Semantic search over text with an embedding index

Retrieval is the backbone of RAG. Pixeltable lets you attach an embedding index to a column and run similarity search with a single call -- the embeddings are computed and indexed for you as data arrives.

import pixeltable as pxt

pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

t = pxt.create_table("demo.docs", {"id": pxt.Int, "text": pxt.String})
t.insert([
    {"id": 1, "text": "Pixeltable stores and transforms multimodal data."},
    {"id": 2, "text": "Cats are small domesticated carnivorous mammals."},
    {"id": 3, "text": "Vector search finds semantically similar documents."},
    {"id": 4, "text": "The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris, France."},
])

# A simple, deterministic, offline embedding so the demo needs no model files.
@pxt.udf
def toy_embed(s: str) -> pxt.Array[(16,), pxt.Float]:
    import numpy as np
    v = np.zeros(16, dtype=np.float32)
    for tok in s.lower().split():
        v[hash(tok) % 16] += 1.0
    n = np.linalg.norm(v)
    return v / n if n else v

t.add_embedding_index(column="text", string_embed=toy_embed)

sim = t.text.similarity("searching for similar text with vectors")
print(t.order_by(sim, asc=False).select(t.id, t.text, score=sim).limit(2).collect())

Run it and you get real output — here's the actual result captured from this exact snippet (ran in 5.77s):

id                                               text     score
0   1  Pixeltable stores and transforms multimodal data.  0.666667
1   4      The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris, France.  0.516398

That's not a mockup. The code above executed against Pixeltable and produced that table. Swap the toy pieces (a UDF, a local embedding) for production ones — a real model endpoint, a managed embedding — and the shape of your code doesn't change. That's the point.

Takeaways

  • Declarative beats imperative for data + AI: say what you want computed, not how to schedule it.
  • Computed columns stay in sync automatically as new rows land.
  • Multimodal is first-class — images, video, audio, and text live in the same table as your scalars.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Then drop the snippet above into a module and run it. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Quick Tip: Benchmarking Multimodal APIs in Under 10 Minutes
  • Beyond Transcription - Multimodal Video and Audio Analysis with Snowflake AI_COMPLETE
  • RAG Series (23): Multimodal RAG — Images and Tables Can Be Retrieved Too
  • Multimodal Search
  • NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Nano Omni: Multimodal Agent Dev Guide

item.title: How to Handle How we index images for RAG with Pixeltable
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using image — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using image — with real, executed output.

How we index images for RAG keeps showing up in the feeds I track — and for good reason. The hard part is rarely the idea; it's the plumbing: wiring data, transforms, and models together without it turning into a pile of glue scripts. Recurring themes around this topic: image, rag, here, claude, code, anthropic.

This is exactly where Pixeltable earns its keep. It gives you a declarative table where derived values are computed columns — define the transform once and it stays in sync as data arrives. No DAGs to wire, no batch jobs to babysit.

A multimodal image table with computed columns

Pixeltable treats images as first-class data. Here we build a table of images, then add computed columns that derive new values automatically -- no manual loops, no glue code. New rows get the same transforms for free.

import pixeltable as pxt
from PIL import Image

# A fresh, isolated workspace.
pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

# Generate a few local images so the example is fully offline.
import tempfile, os
tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
paths = []
for i, color in enumerate([(220, 50, 50), (50, 180, 90), (60, 90, 220)]):
    p = os.path.join(tmp, f"img_{i}.png")
    Image.new("RGB", (256 + i * 32, 192), color).save(p)
    paths.append(p)

# Declare a table with an image column and insert the rows.
t = pxt.create_table("demo.images", {"img": pxt.Image, "label": pxt.String})
t.insert([{"img": p, "label": l} for p, l in zip(paths, ["red", "green", "blue"])])

# Computed columns: derived automatically and kept in sync on insert.
t.add_computed_column(width=t.img.width)
t.add_computed_column(height=t.img.height)
t.add_computed_column(thumb=t.img.resize([96, 96]))

print(t.select(t.label, t.width, t.height).collect())

Run it and you get real output — here's the actual result captured from this exact snippet (ran in 9.84s):

label  width  height
0    red    256     192
1  green    288     192
2   blue    320     192

That's not a mockup. The code above executed against Pixeltable and produced that table. Swap the toy pieces (a UDF, a local embedding) for production ones — a real model endpoint, a managed embedding — and the shape of your code doesn't change. That's the point.

Takeaways

  • Declarative beats imperative for data + AI: say what you want computed, not how to schedule it.
  • Computed columns stay in sync automatically as new rows land.
  • Multimodal is first-class — images, video, audio, and text live in the same table as your scalars.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Then drop the snippet above into a module and run it. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Anthropic's Agents Now Self-Improve Between Sessions. Here's How Dreaming Works.
  • OpenAI's New Realtime Voice Models Can Think, Translate, and Transcribe — Here's What Developers Need to Know
  • Image SEO
  • Stop Asking Claude Code for Markdown Specs. Ask for HTML Artifacts.
  • Codex v0.128.0: /goal Keeps Working Until It's Done -- Even Across Sessions

item.title: ProtoAda: Prototype-Guided Adaptive Adapter Expansion and Geometric Consolidation for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.

ProtoAda: Prototype-Guided Adaptive Adapter Expansion and Geometric Consolidation for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning keeps showing up in the feeds I track — and for good reason. The hard part is rarely the idea; it's the plumbing: wiring data, transforms, and models together without it turning into a pile of glue scripts. Recurring themes around this topic: multimodal, image, llm, video, rag, step-by-step.

This is exactly where Pixeltable earns its keep. It gives you a declarative table where derived values are computed columns — define the transform once and it stays in sync as data arrives. No DAGs to wire, no batch jobs to babysit.

A multimodal image table with computed columns

Pixeltable treats images as first-class data. Here we build a table of images, then add computed columns that derive new values automatically -- no manual loops, no glue code. New rows get the same transforms for free.

import pixeltable as pxt
from PIL import Image

# A fresh, isolated workspace.
pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

# Generate a few local images so the example is fully offline.
import tempfile, os
tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
paths = []
for i, color in enumerate([(220, 50, 50), (50, 180, 90), (60, 90, 220)]):
    p = os.path.join(tmp, f"img_{i}.png")
    Image.new("RGB", (256 + i * 32, 192), color).save(p)
    paths.append(p)

# Declare a table with an image column and insert the rows.
t = pxt.create_table("demo.images", {"img": pxt.Image, "label": pxt.String})
t.insert([{"img": p, "label": l} for p, l in zip(paths, ["red", "green", "blue"])])

# Computed columns: derived automatically and kept in sync on insert.
t.add_computed_column(width=t.img.width)
t.add_computed_column(height=t.img.height)
t.add_computed_column(thumb=t.img.resize([96, 96]))

print(t.select(t.label, t.width, t.height).collect())

Run it and you get real output — here's the actual result captured from this exact snippet (ran in 9.28s):

label  width  height
0    red    256     192
1  green    288     192
2   blue    320     192

That's not a mockup. The code above executed against Pixeltable and produced that table. Swap the toy pieces (a UDF, a local embedding) for production ones — a real model endpoint, a managed embedding — and the shape of your code doesn't change. That's the point.

Takeaways

  • Declarative beats imperative for data + AI: say what you want computed, not how to schedule it.
  • Computed columns stay in sync automatically as new rows land.
  • Multimodal is first-class — images, video, audio, and text live in the same table as your scalars.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Then drop the snippet above into a module and run it. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Quick Tip: Benchmarking Multimodal APIs in Under 10 Minutes
  • Beyond Transcription - Multimodal Video and Audio Analysis with Snowflake AI_COMPLETE
  • RAG Series (23): Multimodal RAG — Images and Tables Can Be Retrieved Too
  • Multimodal Search
  • NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Nano Omni: Multimodal Agent Dev Guide

item.title: Launch HN: Expanse (YC P26) – Unlock Wasted GPU Capacity, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using multimodal — with real, executed output.

Expanse's YC launch put a spotlight on something most teams already feel: GPU capacity is not the bottleneck anymore. The bottleneck is the glue code between your data and the models that consume it. Every multimodal pipeline -- whether it's embedding video frames for RAG, routing images through an LLM agent, or indexing audio for search -- follows the same pattern: load data, transform it, derive new columns, keep everything in sync. And every team writes that plumbing from scratch, poorly, in a tangle of scripts that nobody wants to maintain.

Pixeltable replaces that tangle with a single abstraction: a declarative table where transforms are computed columns. You define the derivation once. New rows get the same transforms automatically. No DAG orchestrator, no batch scheduler, no glue.

Build a multimodal image table in 20 lines

The best way to see what this means is to run it. The example below creates a table of images, then adds computed columns that extract width, height, and a thumbnail -- all derived automatically from the source column. No loops, no manual bookkeeping.

import pixeltable as pxt
from PIL import Image

# A fresh, isolated workspace.
pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

# Generate a few local images so the example is fully offline.
import tempfile, os
tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
paths = []
for i, color in enumerate([(220, 50, 50), (50, 180, 90), (60, 90, 220)]):
    p = os.path.join(tmp, f"img_{i}.png")
    Image.new("RGB", (256 + i * 32, 192), color).save(p)
    paths.append(p)

# Declare a table with an image column and insert the rows.
t = pxt.create_table("demo.images", {"img": pxt.Image, "label": pxt.String})
t.insert([{"img": p, "label": l} for p, l in zip(paths, ["red", "green", "blue"])])

# Computed columns: derived automatically and kept in sync on insert.
t.add_computed_column(width=t.img.width)
t.add_computed_column(height=t.img.height)
t.add_computed_column(thumb=t.img.resize([96, 96]))

print(t.select(t.label, t.width, t.height).collect())

Here is the actual output from running that snippet:

label  width  height
0    red    256     192
1  green    288     192
2   blue    320     192

That is not a mockup. The code executed, the table was created, and those are the real values. The interesting part is what happens next: insert a fourth row and Pixeltable computes width, height, and thumb for it without you asking. The column definitions are the pipeline.

Why this matters for the GPU-capacity conversation

Expanse and projects like it are making raw compute cheaper and more available. That shifts the constraint. When GPU hours are abundant, the limiting factor becomes how fast you can get data into and out of models -- and how reliably your transforms stay consistent as data changes. This is exactly the problem computed columns solve.

Swap the toy transforms above for real ones -- a CLIP embedding column, a frame-extraction UDF over video, an LLM classification call -- and the shape of the code stays identical. You declare the column, Pixeltable handles execution, caching, and incremental updates. The same table can hold images, video, audio, and text alongside scalar metadata. Multimodal is not a special mode; it is the default.

Where to go from here

Install and run the example above in under a minute:

pip install pixeltable

From there, the practical next steps: add an embedding column backed by a real model, attach a similarity index, and query it. The docs walk through each of those. The underlying point stays the same -- stop writing orchestration code and start declaring what you want computed.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Quick Tip: Benchmarking Multimodal APIs in Under 10 Minutes
  • Beyond Transcription - Multimodal Video and Audio Analysis with Snowflake AI_COMPLETE
  • RAG Series (23): Multimodal RAG — Images and Tables Can Be Retrieved Too
  • Multimodal Search
  • NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Nano Omni: Multimodal Agent Dev Guide

item.title: Linear Scaling Video VLMs for Long Video Understanding, the Pixeltable Way
item.description: A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using video — with real, executed output.
item.content:encoded:
A hands-on, runnable walkthrough using video — with real, executed output.

Long video understanding hit an inflection point in 2026. Linear-scaling Video VLMs — models that grow their context proportionally rather than quadratically with frame count — finally made hour-long video inference practical without melting your GPU budget. Between Veo pushing generation quality forward and ffmpeg remaining the universal knife for frame extraction, the tooling side of video AI is maturing fast. But the infrastructure between "I have frames" and "I have answers" remains a mess of bespoke scripts, and that gap grows wider as fine-tuning and inference pipelines get more complex.

The core problem isn't the model. It's everything around it: extracting frames, resizing them, routing them through embeddings or VLMs, storing results alongside the source material, and keeping all of it consistent when you add new data. Most teams end up with a tangle of shell scripts piping ffmpeg output into Python glue that writes to three different stores. It works until it doesn't.

Pixeltable takes a different approach. It gives you a declarative table where transforms are computed columns — define them once, and they stay in sync as data arrives. No DAGs, no orchestrators, no batch jobs to babysit. The same pattern works whether your column holds a scalar, an image, a video clip, or a model output.

Computed columns in practice

The concept is easier to show than explain. Here we build a multimodal image table, then add computed columns that derive new values automatically. Every future insert gets the same transforms with zero additional code.

import pixeltable as pxt
from PIL import Image

# A fresh, isolated workspace.
pxt.drop_dir("demo", force=True)
pxt.create_dir("demo")

# Generate a few local images so the example is fully offline.
import tempfile, os
tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
paths = []
for i, color in enumerate([(220, 50, 50), (50, 180, 90), (60, 90, 220)]):
    p = os.path.join(tmp, f"img_{i}.png")
    Image.new("RGB", (256 + i * 32, 192), color).save(p)
    paths.append(p)

# Declare a table with an image column and insert the rows.
t = pxt.create_table("demo.images", {"img": pxt.Image, "label": pxt.String})
t.insert([{"img": p, "label": l} for p, l in zip(paths, ["red", "green", "blue"])])

# Computed columns: derived automatically and kept in sync on insert.
t.add_computed_column(width=t.img.width)
t.add_computed_column(height=t.img.height)
t.add_computed_column(thumb=t.img.resize([96, 96]))

print(t.select(t.label, t.width, t.height).collect())

Here is the actual output from running that snippet:

label  width  height
0    red    256     192
1  green    288     192
2   blue    320     192

That's real execution, not a mockup. The width, height, and thumb columns were derived automatically at insert time. Add a thousand more rows tomorrow and every one gets the same transforms without touching the pipeline.

Why this matters for video workflows

Now picture the video version. Replace the image column with a video column. Replace t.img.width with a computed column that calls ffmpeg to extract keyframes, another that runs each frame through a VLM for captioning, another that generates embeddings for retrieval. The shape of the code stays the same — declare the table, define the transforms, insert data. Pixeltable handles execution order, caching, and incremental updates.

This is the piece that linear-scaling VLMs need on the infrastructure side. A model that can process an hour of video in linear time is only useful if the pipeline feeding it frames, metadata, and context can keep up without collapsing into a maintenance burden. Declarative computed columns solve that: you describe what you want derived, and the system figures out how and when to compute it.

Try it yourself

pip install pixeltable

Drop the snippet above into a script and run it. Swap the toy images for video frames, the resize for a model call, and you have the skeleton of a production pipeline. Full docs: https://docs.pixeltable.com.

Researched against what's currently driving attention on this topic:

  • Gemini Omni makes video generation feel more like editing
  • Claude Code Video Skills: A Developer's Practical Guide to All 6 Options (2026)
  • Veo 4 Release Date: 70% Odds for Google I/O 2026 (Veo 3.1 Lite Live)
  • I tested 5 managed video APIs back-to-back — here's the rig and what shipped
  • Veo 4 Doesn't Exist Yet, But People Are Already Selling It
